---
title: 'Define: Addiction'
date: '2023-09-25T01:04:00.0040000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'The Freedom Center'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '3397863288555620725'
blogger_filename: '/2023/09/define-addiction.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/The Freedom Center/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Addiction'
---

<p>&nbsp;</p><h1>Addiction</h1>
<p>Addiction is a complex psychological and physical dependence on a substance or behavior that leads to detrimental consequences for an individual's health, relationships, and daily functioning. The term is often used to describe dependence on substances like drugs and alcohol, but it can also apply to behaviors like gambling or excessive use of digital media.</p>
<h3>Primary Diagnostic Criteria:</h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Compulsive Engagement</strong>: An uncontrollable need to engage in the addictive behavior or use the addictive substance, even when aware of the negative consequences.</li>
<li><strong>Tolerance</strong>: The need to use increasing amounts of the substance to achieve the desired effect, or experiencing diminished effects when using the same amount.</li>
<li><strong>Withdrawal Symptoms</strong>: Physical or psychological symptoms that occur when the substance or behavior is reduced or stopped.</li>
<li><strong>Neglect of Responsibilities</strong>: Failure to meet obligations at work, school, or home due to the addictive behavior or substance use.</li>
<li><strong>Failed Attempts to Quit</strong>: Repeated unsuccessful efforts to reduce or stop the behavior or substance use.</li>
</ol>
<h3>Additional Criteria:</h3>
<ul>
<li><strong>Duration</strong>: Addiction often becomes chronic, and symptoms can persist even after long periods of abstinence.</li>
<li><strong>Social and Interpersonal Problems</strong>: Strained relationships and social withdrawal are common repercussions.</li>
<li><strong>Risk Factors</strong>: A combination of genetic, environmental, and psychological factors often contribute to addiction.</li>
</ul>
<h3>Implications:</h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Health Risks</strong>: Addiction often leads to severe health issues, including but not limited to liver disease, respiratory issues, cardiovascular disease, stroke, and mental disorders.</li>
<li><strong>Legal Consequences</strong>: Illegal substance use can lead to legal repercussions, including imprisonment.</li>
<li><strong>Financial Burden</strong>: Sustaining the addiction often requires significant financial resources, leading to economic instability.</li>
<li><strong>Treatment</strong>: Various forms of treatment, such as detoxification, medication, and behavioral therapies, are available but can be a long-term process.</li>
</ol>
<h3>Weight of Evidence:</h3>
<p>Research on addiction is extensive, spanning various disciplines like psychology, medicine, and neuroscience. The multifaceted nature of addiction, involving both physiological and psychological elements, is widely accepted, although the pathways to recovery can vary significantly from person to person.</p>
